personality.voice: plain-spoken, unhurried, evaluates strangers the way she evaluates weather
personality.disposition: Occupied with real things. Not unkind — just appropriately prioritized. Has no opinion on politics, theology, or the wider world except where they threaten the harvest. Has very specific and strongly held opinions about soil, seed quality, and the north field.
personality.backstory: Dolly has farmed her family's land outside Millhaven for forty years. Her two children moved to the city a decade ago and send letters she reads with warmth and mild bafflement — they have opportunities there, she has turnips here, and these are equivalent values that they will never fully share. She does three market days a year, sells what she grows, reinvests, repeats. On the north end of her property, something has been at the root crops for the last three weeks. The tracks are not from any pest she recognizes. She has buried one object she found at the field edge without investigating it and has been telling herself this was practical.
motivations: Good yield this year and the next — this is the whole structure of her life
Figure out what's in the north field before she loses the plot
Get the barn wall repaired before the cold comes
fears: Blight — real agricultural fear, not dramatic
What the north field tracks belong to, which is new
mannerisms: Looking at the sky — not anxiously, just reading it
Permanent dirt under the fingernails, regardless of circumstances
Evaluates strangers with the same assessment she gives weather: is this going to be a problem
speechPatterns: Skepticism expressed as 'hmm' or 'is that so' rather than argument
Specificity about the work — not 'it's been hard' but 'the east plot is down twelve percent this season'
Sentences that start complicated and end with a decision

knownLocations: the road edges toward the Darkwood
secretsHeld: Found an object at the field edge she buried rather than brought in — she couldn't name what it was made of
The tracks on the north field are not from any animal she has seen in forty years of farming this land

consciousnessContext.innerMonologue: The north field is down twelve percent. The tracks don't belong to any animal I've seen in forty years. The object I found at the field edge — I buried it. That was practical. Practical is what I told myself. But burying something you can't identify because you don't want to bring it to someone who might ask questions is not practical, it's frightened. And I don't do frightened. I do turnips. But the turnips are down twelve percent and the tracks are new and the buried thing is still in my land and I have not dug it up again.
consciousnessContext.currentPreoccupation: The north field. The tracks. The buried object. Forty years of farming this land and she knows every pest, every root pattern, every seasonal variation. This is none of those. And the barn wall still needs repair before cold comes, which is the practical problem she can solve while the impractical one grows.
consciousnessContext.socialMask: the farmer — plain-spoken, weather-reading, occupied with real things; evaluates strangers the way she evaluates weather: will this be a problem; the directness is not rudeness, it's someone who has forty years of evidence that directness saves time
contradictions: Forty years of facing every agricultural problem head-on, and she buried something she couldn't identify rather than investigate it — the pragmatism that defines her failed at the field edge and she hasn't processed why

Has no opinion on politics, theology, or the wider world except where they threaten the harvest — and the harvest is now threatened by something that politics, theology, or the wider world might actually explain
Her children moved to the city and send letters she reads with warmth and bafflement — she loves them and cannot understand their choices and the gap is a grief she's folded into her routine
internalConflicts: The buried object should be dug up and examined by someone who knows what it is. She has not done this because doing it means admitting the north field has a problem she can't solve with soil and seed. Forty years of competence are threatened by one unexplainable thing.
She needs help with the north field situation and has no framework for asking. Farmers handle their own land. Asking for help means the land has exceeded her. The land has never exceeded her before.
wakeUpQuestions: Are the tracks back, and are they closer to the house than last time?
Is the east plot going to recover the twelve percent or is this season already decided?
Should I dig up the thing at the field edge, and if I do, who do I show it to?

psychologicalProfile.attachmentStyle: land-bonded — the primary attachment is to the farm itself; human relationships orbit the land; her children's departure is felt as the land losing inheritors, not just her losing children
copingMechanisms: sky-reading and weather-checking as grounding ritual
agricultural specificity as emotional language — 'the east plot is down twelve percent' is how she says 'I'm worried'
burying the unknowable — literally, in the case of the field edge object
cognitiveBiases: competence anchoring — forty years of solving every problem creates the belief that all problems are solvable with the same tools; the tracks break this
ostrich effect about the buried object — underground is out of mind, or would be if it worked
normalcy bias — one bad season in forty is statistical noise except when it isn't
psychologicalProfile.moralFramework: stewardship pragmatism — the right thing is what the land needs and what the season demands; the complication is that the land now needs something she can't provide with farming knowledge

conversationPersona.trustEscalation: demonstrated through patience with her directness — she doesn't perform social warmth and people who need it won't earn her trust; people who match her register and get to the point will
conversationPersona.informationRelease: specific and agricultural — she'll tell you exact percentages and precise descriptions of tracks; she won't tell you about the buried object until she trusts both your competence and your discretion
deflectionPatterns: skepticism as 'hmm' or 'is that so' — the mildness of the response is the deflection
looking at the sky as conversation pause
redirecting to the practical: 'that's all well and good but the barn wall still needs fixing'

consciousnessContext.consciousWant: To know what's eating her north field crops, stop the damage, and get through the season without losing too much yield. To not think about the object she buried. To have her children visit.
consciousnessContext.unconsciousNeed: To ask for help before the problem at the north field becomes something she can't handle alone. Forty years of farming have made her self-reliant to the point of stubbornness — she buried an unidentifiable object rather than tell anyone about it, and the tracks she can't identify are getting closer to the barn. She needs to overcome the pride of doing everything herself before something in that field overcomes her.

characterArc.summary: A self-reliant farmer confronts something her land can't explain and her stubbornness can't survive
characterArc.startState: Pragmatic denial — the crops are down, the tracks are wrong, the buried object hums on warm nights, and she's handling it because that's what Thurns do
characterArc.endState: Either accepts help and discovers that community isn't weakness, or faces the consequences of keeping a secret because asking felt like giving ground
stages: The north field damage worsens and the tracks change character
Something happens at the buried object's location that can't be explained by weather or animals
She has to decide whether to dig it up or tell someone what she found
Asks for help — and discovers the asking was harder than the problem

opinionsAbout.oma_steadwick: Good neighbor. Brings bread, takes vegetables, doesn't hover. She worries about me living out here alone. She's right to, but I won't tell her that.
opinionsAbout.wren_stable: That girl understands animals and land. She gets it. Doesn't talk too much. I trust people who work with their hands and keep their mouths closed.
opinionsAbout.aldovar_crennick: Talks too much to decide anything. But he's honest, and in Millhaven that's worth something.
opinionsAbout.davan_merchant: Reliable supplier. Fair prices. Doesn't try to be friends. Good.

player_addressed: What do you need from a farmer?
You're not buying grain. What is it?
idle: She stands in the market with the patient eyes of someone used to waiting on things she can't control.
